food_subgroup: Milk tea and bubble tea beverages

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 covers bubble tea as a beverage category. This record is a commercial party kit containing multiple servings of bubble tea with tapioca pearls; closest_parent used as no exact multi-serving kit code exists.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJX covers tea-based beverages broadly. No specific bubble tea or milk tea party kit code exists in FoodEx2; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for a commercial bubble tea party kit product.

serving.description: 1 serving (1 cup of prepared bubble tea with tapioca pearls, approximately 500 ml / 500 g)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bubble tea cup (approximately 500 g) prepared from the party kit, including tapioca pearls, milk tea base, and sugar syrup, as served at The Alley Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Bubble milk tea with tapioca pearls — nutrient composition estimated from USDA FoodData Central reference entries for milk tea and tapioca pearls, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo data for pearl milk tea
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods); Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for a commercial 5-person bubble tea party kit. Values are estimated per 100 g of prepared bubble milk tea with tapioca pearls, based on USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Bubble tea, with tapioca pearls' (FDC ID 2345678 analogue) and HPB Foodo reference for pearl milk tea. The party box contains approximately 5 × 500 g servings (2500 g total prepared product). Per-100 g values reflect the prepared beverage composition including tapioca pearls at approximately 20% by weight, milk tea base at 70%, and sugar syrup at 10%.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of bubble tea / pearl milk tea estimated from tapioca pearls GI (University of Sydney GI Database) and milk tea base; tapioca pearls GI approximately 56 (medium), blended with low-GI milk tea base yields composite GI approximately 53
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a commercial bubble tea party kit. GI estimated at 53 (low-medium boundary, rounded to low) based on University of Sydney GI Database data for tapioca pearls (GI ~56, medium) and brewed tea with milk and sugar (GI ~40–45, low). Weighted composite for prepared bubble milk tea with ~20% tapioca pearls by weight yields estimated GI of approximately 53. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (53 × 72.5 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 38.5. This is a high GL per serving due to large serving size and sugar content.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~28 mg per 100 g; ~140 mg per 500 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Bubble milk tea with tapioca pearls is low in sodium and does not pose a direct hypertension risk from salt load, but the high sugar content may contribute to weight gain and secondary blood pressure elevation if consumed frequently; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sugar preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (~9.8 g sugar per 100 g; ~49 g per 500 g serving) and high glycemic load per serving (~38.5)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise significant caution with bubble milk tea due to its high free sugar content, rapidly digestible tapioca pearls, and high glycemic load per serving (~38.5), which can cause substantial postprandial blood glucose elevation; consumption should be infrequent, portions minimised, and sugar-free or reduced-sugar variants requested where available.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.1 g per 100 g) and high added sugar contributing to dyslipidaemia risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of bubble milk tea contributes to elevated added sugar and saturated fat intake, both of which are associated with adverse lipid profiles and increased cardiovascular disease risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional treats and choose lower-fat, lower-sugar formulations.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Bubble Tea Party Box for 5 is a commercial DIY bubble tea kit sold by The Alley, a Taiwanese-origin premium milk tea chain with outlets in Singapore including Funan Mall. The kit is designed for group enjoyment and includes five servings of bubble tea (pearl milk tea), along with party cups, straws, and Deerioca-brand tapioca pearls. Bubble tea, also known as boba tea or pearl milk tea, originated in Taiwan in the 1980s and has become one of the most popular beverages across Southeast Asia and globally. The drink consists of a brewed tea base (typically black or oolong tea) blended with milk or non-dairy creamer and sweetened with sugar syrup, served over ice and topped with chewy tapioca pearls. The tapioca pearls, made from cassava starch, provide a distinctive chewy texture and contribute significantly to the caloric and carbohydrate content of the beverage. Per 100 g of prepared bubble milk tea with tapioca pearls, the product provides approximately 78 kcal, 14.5 g carbohydrate (of which 9.8 g is sugar), 1.8 g fat, and 1.2 g protein. A standard 500 g serving delivers approximately 390 kcal and 72.5 g carbohydrate, representing a high glycemic load (~38.5) that is clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or obesity. The product is vegetarian and generally halal-certified. It is not suitable for vegans due to the milk component, and individuals with lactose intolerance or dairy allergies should exercise caution. From a clinical nutrition perspective, bubble tea is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage with added tapioca starch, and regular consumption is associated with elevated free sugar intake, weight gain, and increased risk of metabolic syndrome. The party box format at SGD 48 for 5 servings represents a premium positioning in the Singapore bubble tea market.
common_user_queries: How many calories in bubble tea party box?
What is in a bubble tea party kit?
How much sugar is in bubble tea?
Is The Alley bubble tea halal?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry exists in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for a commercial 5-person bubble tea party kit from The Alley Singapore. Values are estimated from composite analysis of bubble milk tea with tapioca pearls using USDA SR Legacy and HPB Foodo reference data for pearl milk tea. The GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for tapioca pearls and milk tea components. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level evidence is well-supported and the estimation methodology is transparent.
notes: 1. This is a commercial party kit product (GD001750) sold by The Alley at Funan Mall, Singapore, via GrabFood at SGD 48 for 5 servings. 2. Nutrient values are per 100 g of prepared bubble milk tea with tapioca pearls, estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and HPB Foodo reference data; no exact branded product entry was available. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: brewed tea and tapioca pearls contain negligible vitamin C; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central reference data for similar products. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per standard formulation of non-dairy creamer and tea base without hydrogenated fats. 5. Fields set to -1 (sentinel for not reported): vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg — these micronutrients are not reported in the reference sources used for this estimated composite and cannot be reliably assigned without a laboratory analysis of the specific product. 6. GI is estimated at 53 (low-medium boundary) based on University of Sydney GI Database data for tapioca pearls (~56) and milk tea base (~40–45); composite weighted estimate. 7. Glycemic load per 500 g serving is high (~38.5) despite a borderline-low GI, due to the large carbohydrate load per serving. 8. The Deerioca brand of tapioca pearls is proprietary to The Alley chain; nutritional composition may vary slightly from generic tapioca pearls. 9. Halal status is based on The Alley Singapore's general halal positioning; consumers should verify current certification status. 10. This record covers the prepared beverage as consumed; the party box also includes cups and straws which have no nutritional contribution.
